nativeRange.habitat: Shallow margins of ponds, slow-moving streams, and seasonally flooded wetlands. Eleocharis pusilla is a temperate Australasian spikerush found across damp soils and areas of shallow standing water in Australia and New Zealand.

decorPreferences: fine sand or small-grain substrate to anchor delicate roots
open foreground with strong direct light
stable, clean water column with gentle flow

avoidWith: boisterous fish that uproot fine carpet plants

imageCandidates.notes: Retailer product page photo of tissue-culture Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini' carpet. Watermarked retailer imagery, used as a visual candidate only.

summary: Mini Dwarf Hairgrass (Eleocharis pusilla) is one of the smallest carpeting plants in the hobby, distinctly shorter and finer than the more common Dwarf Hairgrass. Mature blades stay around 3–5 cm tall, forming a dense, lawn-like foreground mat. It is generally considered demanding outside well-equipped, high-tech setups.
careNotes:
Mini Dwarf Hairgrass is a small Australasian spikerush in the sedge family (Cyperaceae) that the aquarium trade markets as the 'true dwarf' hairgrass. It is distinctly shorter and finer than the more common Eleocharis parvula (Dwarf Hairgrass) and considerably more compact than the larger Eleocharis acicularis (Needle Spikerush). Mature blades typically stay around 3–5 cm tall, making it one of the smallest hairgrasses available and well suited to foreground carpeting in iwagumi and nature-style aquascapes where fine-scale ground cover matters.

Care is generally considered demanding. Pressurised CO2 (around 15–25 mg/L) combined with strong direct light over the foreground is widely recommended to keep the carpet compact and prevent it from melting back or being overrun by algae during establishment. A fine, nutrient-rich substrate such as aquasoil supports the delicate root system; supplementing with root tabs and a balanced dose of macro nutrients and trace iron is commonly advised. Water temperatures of 20–27 °C, a pH of around 6.0–7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (gH around 2–10) all suit this species. It tolerates a fairly wide hardness range but tends to perform best in soft, slightly acidic water.

The dry-start method — establishing the plants emersed on damp aquasoil under a sealed lid for four to eight weeks before flooding the tank — is widely used to build a dense, well-rooted carpet from the outset. Once the carpet is established, trimming needs are minimal given the plant's naturally small mature height; occasional light pruning keeps the mat tidy and allows light to reach the lower blades. This species is best treated as an intermediate-to-advanced plant and is generally not recommended as a first carpet plant unless CO2, lighting, and nutrient levels can be held consistently stable.
breedingNotes: Mini Dwarf Hairgrass propagates vegetatively by spreading horizontally via short runners called stolons, which is how a planted cluster gradually thickens into a full foreground carpet over weeks to months. Aquascapers most commonly start from tissue-culture cups, dividing the plug into many small clumps of a few stems each and spacing them 2–4 cm apart so the runners can knit the gaps together. The dry-start method — growing the plants emersed on damp substrate under a sealed lid before flooding the tank — is widely used to establish a dense, well-rooted carpet from the beginning. No sexual propagation is used in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Retailer product page for tissue-culture Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini'. Source for adult height (~1–2 in / 3–5 cm), medium-to-high lighting requirement, recommended CO2 supplementation, slow growth rate, foreground/carpet placement, nutrient-rich substrate, and propagation by separating tissue-culture plugs that spread by runners.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Eleocharis
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy (family Cyperaceae, order Poales — the sedge family within the grass-like Poales), the broadly aquatic-to-mesic 'marsh dweller' habitat profile (sea level to 5,000+ m), the confirmation that Eleocharis pusilla R.Br. is a recognised species in the genus, and disambiguation against the better-known hobby species E. acicularis (Needle Spikerush) and E. parvula (Dwarf Spikerush / Dwarf Hairgrass) for which pusilla is the smaller cousin.
